content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 6 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 6 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 6:1 -- "grievance / matter / dispute" and "unrighteous / secular court"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Does anyone of you dare, having a matter against the other, to be judged before the unrighteous and not before the holy [ones]?" | pragma as "matter"; adikoi as "unrighteous" |
| LOGOS | "When one of you has a grievance against another, does he dare go to law before the unrighteous instead of the saints?" | pragma as "grievance"; adikoi as "unrighteous" |
| PNEUMA | "When one of you has a dispute with another believer, how dare you file a lawsuit and ask a secular court to decide the matter instead of taking it to other believers!" | pragma as "dispute"; adikoi as "secular court"; adds "file a lawsuit," "believer" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Grievance" effectively captures pragma in a legal context. "Unrighteous" for adikoi preserves the wordplay Paul uses throughout the passage (adikoi in v.1 connects to adikeo in vv.7-8 and adikoi in v.9). PNEUMA's "secular court" loses this wordplay entirely and modernizes the reference.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The adikoi wordplay noted as important.

---

## 6:2 -- "incompetent / unworthy"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "are you unworthy of the smallest judgments?" | anaxioi as "unworthy" |
| LOGOS | "are you incompetent to try trivial cases?" | anaxioi as "incompetent" |
| PNEUMA | "can't you decide even these little things among yourselves?" | Restructures entirely; drops anaxioi |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Incompetent" captures the sense of anaxioi (not up to the task) in a judicial context better than "unworthy" (EMET, which has moral overtones). PNEUMA's restructuring loses the specific term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:4 -- Ambiguous verse: "those who have no standing" vs. "the despised ones you seat as judges"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [ones] having been despised in the assembly -- these you seat [as judges]?" | Reads as rhetorical question: you appoint the despised as judges? (ironic) |
| LOGOS | "why do you lay them before those who have no standing in the church?" | Reads as: why go to those outside the church? |
| PNEUMA | "why go to outside judges who are not respected by the church?" | "outside judges"; "not respected" for exoutheneo |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. This verse is notoriously ambiguous in Greek. The exoutheneomenous ("despised/of no account") could refer to (a) pagan judges whom the church should consider unqualified, or (b) lower-status church members whom Paul ironically suggests could still handle such cases. LOGOS takes interpretation (a), which fits the flow of the argument. EMET's rendering preserves the ambiguity. PNEUMA's "outside judges" resolves it too quickly.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's ambiguity-preserving approach acknowledged.

---

## 6:7 -- "defeat / completely defeated"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Already then it is altogether a defeat for you that you have lawsuits with one another" | hettema as "defeat" |
| LOGOS | "To have lawsuits at all with one another is already a defeat for you" | Same core term |
| PNEUMA | "Even to have such lawsuits with one another is a defeat for you" | Slight variation; "even" for emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. All three are quite close here. LOGOS's word order ("To have lawsuits at all...is already a defeat") best mirrors the rhetorical weight Paul places on the mere existence of lawsuits.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 6:9 -- "men who practice homosexuality / effeminate, males who lie with males / male prostitutes, practice homosexuality"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "neither sexually immoral, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor effeminate, nor males who lie with males" | malakoi as "effeminate"; arsenokoitai as "males who lie with males" (etymological) |
| LOGOS | "neither the sexually immoral, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor men who practice homosexuality" | Combines malakoi + arsenokoitai into one phrase |
| PNEUMA | "Those who indulge in sexual sin, or who worship idols, or commit adultery, or are male prostitutes, or practice homosexuality" | malakoi as "male prostitutes"; arsenokoitai as "practice homosexuality" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's combined rendering as a practical solution, but with significant reservation. The two Greek terms malakoi and arsenokoitai are among the most debated in Pauline scholarship. EMET's approach (translating each separately: "effeminate" and "males who lie with males") is the most transparent, preserving both terms for the reader. PNEUMA's "male prostitutes" for malakoi is one scholarly interpretation but not the only one. LOGOS's combination into "men who practice homosexuality" is efficient but collapses two distinct terms into one modern category. SOPHIA's consensus text follows LOGOS but includes a footnote directing readers to EMET's distinction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS with reservation. This remains one of the most contested translation decisions in the NT. EMET's two-term rendering noted as the most lexically transparent.

---

## 6:11 -- "washed / cleansed" and triple construction

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "But you were washed, but you were sanctified, but you were justified in the name of the Lord Yeshua Christos and in the Spirit of the God of us" | Preserves triple alla ("but"); apolouo as "washed" |
| LOGOS | "But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God" | Single "but"; apolouo as "washed" |
| PNEUMA | "But you were cleansed; you were made holy; you were made right with God by calling on the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God" | apolouo as "cleansed"; hagiazo as "made holy"; dikaioo as "made right with God"; adds "by calling on" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Washed, sanctified, justified" preserves the technical theological triad. PNEUMA's "cleansed, made holy, made right with God" is more accessible but loses the established English theological vocabulary. PNEUMA's addition of "by calling on" is not in the Greek -- the name and Spirit are the instruments, not the invocation. EMET's triple "but" (alla) preserves the emphatic Greek structure but reads awkwardly in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:12 -- "lawful / permitted / allowed" and "helpful / beneficial / good for you" and "dominated / mastered / slave"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "'All [things] are permitted to me' -- but not all [things] are beneficial. 'All [things] are permitted to me' -- but I will not be mastered by anything" | exestin as "permitted"; symphero as "beneficial"; exousiazo as "mastered" |
| LOGOS | "'All things are lawful for me,' but not all things are helpful. 'All things are lawful for me,' but I will not be dominated by anything" | exestin as "lawful"; symphero as "helpful"; exousiazo as "dominated" |
| PNEUMA | "You say, 'I am allowed to do anything' -- but not everything is good for you. And even though 'I am allowed to do anything,' I must not become a slave to anything" | exestin as "allowed"; symphero as "good for you"; exousiazo as "become a slave to"; adds "You say" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Lawful" for exestin captures the rights-language Paul is engaging. "Helpful" for symphero is more specific than "good for you" (PNEUMA). "Dominated" for exousiazo preserves the power/authority (exousia) wordplay. PNEUMA's "become a slave to" is interpretively effective but loses the exousia root connection. PNEUMA's "You say" makes explicit what LOGOS indicates with quotation marks.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:13 -- "Food is meant for the stomach / The foods for the belly"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "'The foods for the belly and the belly for the foods' -- but God will destroy both this and these" | Preserves slogan form; plural "foods" |
| LOGOS | "'Food is meant for the stomach and the stomach for food' -- and God will destroy both one and the other" | Smooths syntax; "stomach" for koilia |
| PNEUMA | "You say, 'Food was made for the stomach, and the stomach for food.' This is true, though someday God will do away with both of them" | Adds "You say," "This is true," "someday"; "do away with" for katargeo |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "This is true" is an interpretive addition -- Paul's relationship to this slogan is complex (he partially agrees but redirects it). Adding "This is true" implies full endorsement. "Do away with" weakens katargeo (abolish, render inoperative) to something more casual.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:15 -- "Never! / May it not be! / Never!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "May it not be!" | me genoito as classical rendering |
| LOGOS | "Never!" | Emphatic English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Never!" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Never!" effectively conveys the vehemence of me genoito. EMET's "May it not be!" is more literal but archaic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 6:18 -- "Flee from sexual immorality / Flee the sexual immorality / Run from sexual sin"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Flee the sexual immorality. Every sin which a man may commit is outside the body" | Direct object without preposition |
| LOGOS | "Flee from sexual immorality. Every other sin a person commits is outside the body" | Adds "from" and "other" |
| PNEUMA | "Run from sexual sin! No other sin so clearly affects the body as this one does" | "Run" for pheugo; major expansion of the second clause |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Flee from" is natural English for pheugo + accusative. The addition of "other" in "every other sin" is interpretive but warranted by context (Paul is distinguishing sexual sin from other sins). PNEUMA's extensive restructuring of the second clause ("No other sin so clearly affects the body as this one does") replaces Paul's statement with an interpretive paraphrase that reverses the syntax.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:19 -- "temple of the Holy Spirit within you / temple of the Holy Spirit, who lives in you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the body of you is [the] temple of the Holy Spirit in you, which you have from God, and you are not your own?" | en hymin as "in you"; relative pronoun preserved |
| LOGOS | "your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you, whom you have from God? You are not your own" | "within you"; personal "whom" for Spirit |
| PNEUMA | "your body is the temple of the Holy Spirit, who lives in you and was given to you by God? You do not belong to yourself" | Adds "who lives in"; "given to you" for "you have from God"; "do not belong to yourself" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Within you" clarifies the spatial metaphor. "Whom you have from God" preserves the relative clause structure. PNEUMA's "who lives in you" is dynamic but adds a verb not present. "You are not your own" (LOGOS) is more forceful than "You do not belong to yourself" (PNEUMA) -- the blunt possessive denial mirrors Paul's rhetoric.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:20 -- "bought with a price / bought with a high price"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you were bought with a price. Therefore glorify God in the body of you" | times as "a price"; no adjective |
| LOGOS | "for you were bought with a price. So glorify God in your body" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "for God bought you with a high price. So you must honor God with your body" | Adds "God" as subject; "high" qualifying price; "honor" for doxazo; "must" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Paul says simply "a price" (times) -- PNEUMA's "high price" adds a qualifier not in the Greek (though theologically accurate). "Glorify" (doxazo) is more theologically loaded than "honor" and should be preserved. PNEUMA's "must" adds imperative force beyond the Greek aorist imperative.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA modernizes legal/judicial language | vv.1, 2, 4 | "secular court" for adikoi; "file a lawsuit" |
| PNEUMA adds interpretive qualifiers | vv.11, 13, 20 | "by calling on," "This is true," "high price" |
| Key contested vocabulary (malakoi/arsenokoitai) | v.9 | Three different approaches to two highly debated terms |
| PNEUMA weakens strong Greek verbs | vv.13, 15 | katargeo as "do away with"; pheugo as "run" |
| LOGOS-SOPHIA alignment | Throughout | LOGOS as base text |
| EMET preserves Greek wordplay | v.1 | adikoi chain through chapter |
| PNEUMA restructures Paul's compressed rhetoric | vv.12, 13, 18 | Breaks compressed slogans into expanded explanations |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Chapter 6 contains some of the most debated vocabulary in the Pauline corpus (v.9, malakoi/arsenokoitai) and some of Paul's most compressed rhetorical moves (the slogan-and-response pattern of vv.12-13). PNEUMA's tendency to expand compressed rhetoric into explanatory prose is most problematic in this chapter, where Paul's brevity is itself rhetorically powerful. The slogan quotations ("all things are lawful," "food for the stomach") lose their force when PNEUMA adds "You say" and "This is true" -- Paul's relationship to these slogans is deliberately ambiguous, and PNEUMA resolves the ambiguity prematurely. The most significant translation decision in this chapter is v.9, where all three translators handle the malakoi/arsenokoitai pair differently. SOPHIA acknowledged the difficulty and adopted LOGOS's rendering with an explicit note about EMET's alternative.

---
